sources.notes: Statement and constraints: dynamic multiset of barrel water amounts; identify one poisoned barrel using comparisons and pours only from proven-safe barrels; n,q <= 2e5 and values <= 1e6.
Div.2 duplicate alias; the local database marks cf_problem:2066:E as canonical.
Official editorial page checked; its tutorial body was not visible in text, so the detailed solution was cross-checked with public writeups.
Used to confirm the duplicate safe-mass seed, consuming values <= L, consuming adjacent gaps <= L, and ordered-set or segment-tree maintenance.
Independent writeup used to confirm the known-safe-mass operations and power-of-two bucket implementation.
Independent writeup used to cross-check the special-point/segment-tree closure formulation.

reference_candidates.notes: Luogu solution index checked; the concrete consulted Luogu article is recorded in sources.
Official editorial-referenced accepted submission; direct source access was blocked, so it was not used as evidence.

annotation.summary: Maintain a changing multiset of barrel water amounts and decide whether there is a guaranteed safe strategy to identify the unique poisoned barrel.
annotation.constraints: n,q <= 2e5 and all values are <= 1e6, so each update must be logarithmic or depend only on O(log value) buckets; pours may be fractional.
annotation.core_idea: A comparison is useful only after making two compared barrels have equal water amounts using proven-safe water. If unequal, the heavier barrel is poisoned; in the worst case the comparison is equal and both become safe. Thus the problem reduces to growing a known-safe water mass L. Duplicate values seed L because equal barrels can be compared immediately. Among remaining unique values, any x <= L can be certified using safe water, and any adjacent pair x<y with y-x <= L can be equalized and certified together. Greedily close under these moves; if at least two candidates remain and the minimum adjacent gap is greater than L, no strategy can progress. A successful pair move increases L enough that only logarithmically many stalled phases occur. Dynamic structures maintain singleton values, sums, and adjacent gaps under updates.
annotation.complexity: O((n+q)(log n + log A)) with dyadic buckets, or O((n+q)log^2 A) with a value-domain segment tree, where A <= 1e6.
tricks: reduce adaptive identification to the worst-case equal-comparison branch
known-safe water mass L as the only feasibility state
consume unique values <= L or adjacent pairs with gap <= L
power-of-two buckets make closure phases logarithmic

solution_variants.name: safe-mass-closure-dyadic-buckets
solution_variants.summary: Counts at least two contribute initial safe mass; singleton values live in power-of-two buckets. Each bucket maintains order, sum, extrema, and minimum adjacent gap. Scan buckets from small to large and absorb a prefix whenever current safe mass can certify a value or adjacent pair.
solution_variants.complexity: O(log n) update plus O(log A) answer scan.

solution_variants.name: value-domain-segment-tree-closure
solution_variants.summary: Maintain singleton values over the bounded value domain with range sums and minimum adjacent gaps. Repeatedly add all singleton values <= L, then consume a minimum-gap pair when its gap <= L; restore temporary removals after checking.
solution_variants.complexity: O(log^2 A) amortized per answer and O(log A) per update.

tags.evidence: The reduction follows the branch where every equalized comparison returns equal and no poison is immediately exposed; the strategy must still certify all but one barrel.
Consuming every value <= L and then any closest pair with gap <= L is sufficient; if neither move exists, no safe operation can create new information.
Values are partitioned into power-of-two ranges; after a successful certification in a bucket, safe mass reaches the next threshold and a prefix of buckets is absorbed.
Each dyadic bucket needs dynamic insertion/deletion, extrema, and adjacent-gap maintenance for singleton values.
The alternative maintains singleton sums and minimum adjacent gaps with segment trees under updates.
The certifiable set only expands as L grows, and pair-consumption increases L enough to bound phases by O(log A).
Ordered-set upper_bound or segment-tree descent may locate the next value, but there is no binary-search-on-answer reduction.
Updates have duplicate-count and adjacent-gap boundary cases, but implementation is not the retrieval focus.
